messages.author: danielp
messages.text: @Mike / N2QDK just noticed you are using 907.525 while most use 910.525. Why?
messages.content_html: @Mike / N2QDK just noticed you are using 907.525 while most use 910.525. Why?

messages.author: Mike / N2QDK
messages.text: 1) Moving away from some significant local radio frequency interference (RFI) at a downtown Albany location, and 2) move closer to Meshtastic frequency so cavity filters and other hardware specifically designed for Meshtastic can be utilized for MeshCore (edited)
messages.content_html: 1) Moving away from some significant local radio frequency interference (RFI) at a downtown Albany location, and 2) move closer to Meshtastic frequency so cavity filters and other hardware specifically designed for Meshtastic can be utilized for MeshCore (edited)

messages.author: garrisonsan
messages.text: Glad to see I'm not the only one who came to the same conclusion about frequency and had the same experience with SF10
messages.content_html: Glad to see I'm not the only one who came to the same conclusion about frequency and had the same experience with SF10
messages.reactions_html: 1

messages.author: Mike / N2QDK
messages.text: Oh yeah? I knew there was some discussion around SF, but not frequency defaults
messages.content_html: Oh yeah? I knew there was some discussion around SF, but not frequency defaults

messages.author: danielp
messages.text: I understand the reasoning, but wouldn’t it limit the mesh network effect in the future? The nodes set to different frequencies (and as far as I understand different SF) won’t communicate with one another. (edited)
messages.content_html: I understand the reasoning, but wouldn’t it limit the mesh network effect in the future? The nodes set to different frequencies (and as far as I understand different SF) won’t communicate with one another. (edited)

messages.author: garrisonsan
messages.text: It's been an ongoing discussion (not on this discord), but the first movers in the MC scene in TX decided to go a different route than 910.525. See https://discord.com/channels/1343693475589263471/1356857923535376484 for the details

messages.author: Splicer
messages.text: Howdy folks! New to MC. I just converted two of my MT nodes to MC (one companion, one repeater) to try it out. There's a possibility I could add a repeater to the roof of a building in downtown Troy at my makerspace but right now I've got nothing on any roofs, just have my repeater on top of the tallest bookcase on the second floor at home.
messages.content_html: Howdy folks! New to MC. I just converted two of my MT nodes to MC (one companion, one repeater) to try it out. There's a possibility I could add a repeater to the roof of a building in downtown Troy at my makerspace but right now I've got nothing on any roofs, just have my repeater on top of the tallest bookcase on the second floor at home.
messages.reactions_html: 2

messages.author: Alex / KD2DLL
messages.text: @Mike / N2QDK is kinda spearheading the local group for MC. There's some repeaters scattered around downtown Albany, Guilderland area, and atop Maple Ski Ridge currently
messages.content_html: @Mike / N2QDK is kinda spearheading the local group for MC. There's some repeaters scattered around downtown Albany, Guilderland area, and atop Maple Ski Ridge currently

messages.author: Splicer
messages.text: Thanks! I added my repeater to the map. Fudged the actual location slightly since I didn't see an dilution-of-precision settings. It's showing at an intersection near me, not actually at my house. But I did get my repeater up into the attic.

messages.author: Splicer
messages.text: The makerspace is Tech Valley Center of Gravity in the Quackenbush building in downtown Troy. It's a five-story building but it's not up on the hill. Roof access is possible but not easy, so I have to be ready with a tested self-sustaining node that I'm confident can survive both summers and winters before I put something up there.

The maple repeater isnt quite in its final position yet, as I'm planning to get it upto 20 feet higher at some point

messages.author: Mike / N2QDK
messages.text: My repeaters aren’t pin point accurate, that’s for sure!
messages.content_html: My repeaters aren’t pin point accurate, that’s for sure!

messages.author: Splicer
messages.text: Of course, height makes sense. I think what @Alex / KD2DLL was commenting on was that a little difference north-south made a huge difference even without much difference in height. If you look around the old location on a topo map, it's not perfectly flat but it's pretty darn flat. But to the West of me is a hill. Go a little farther south and it's flat to the West. The spot I picked previously is 3 meters higher than where I actually live. But go West from me and you have 25 more meters up in less than a mile. Go West from the other spot and that same hill is only an additional 5 meters in elevation up. The ski area repeater is what, about 250 meters in altitude? Roughly 150 higher than me. I'm not going to try to do the trigonometry in my head. Point is I'd need an antenna mast a lot more than that 3 meter distance to overcome the hill that I'm near but not on.
messages.content_html: Of course, height makes sense. I think what @Alex / KD2DLL was commenting on was that a little difference north-south made a huge difference even without much difference in height. If you look around the old location on a topo map, it's not perfectly flat but it's pretty darn flat. But to the West of me is a hill. Go a little farther south and it's flat to the West. The spot I picked previously is 3 meters higher than where I actually live. But go West from me and you have 25 more meters up in less than a mile. Go West from the other spot and that same hill is only an additional 5 meters in elevation up. The ski area repeater is what, about 250 meters in altitude? Roughly 150 higher than me. I'm not going to try to do the trigonometry in my head. Point is I'd need an antenna mast a lot more than that 3 meter distance to overcome the hill that I'm near but not on.
messages.reply_html:
Mike / N2QDK
And height

messages.author: Splicer
messages.text: Went through Schenectady and over to Autumn Glow at the Mabee Farm this evening. Brought my companion node, advertised a couple times and posted howdy to the Public Channel. Sent but no repeat heard. At some point I'll go down to Grooms and Vischer Ferry and see if I can pick up Maple Ridge from there. (Or maybe tomorrow I'll take a walk up by the water tower.)

messages.author: Splicer
messages.text: I want to say it was super gratifying to have an actual (if brief) conversation over LoRa Mesh this morning. The very first I've had with people I didn't know IRL and wasn't in the same room with. It says good things about MC; I've been doing MT for seven months. I've sent and received messages on MT but never a complete exchange where I got a reply to a reply and knew messages had made the round trip. Was that you @Mike / N2QDK ?
messages.content_html: I want to say it was super gratifying to have an actual (if brief) conversation over LoRa Mesh this morning. The very first I've had with people I didn't know IRL and wasn't in the same room with. It says good things about MC; I've been doing MT for seven months. I've sent and received messages on MT but never a complete exchange where I got a reply to a reply and knew messages had made the round trip. Was that you @Mike / N2QDK?

messages.author: Mike / N2QDK
messages.text: it was!
messages.content_html: it was!

messages.author: Splicer
messages.text: I was surprised, considering the (allegedly) broader adoption of MT that my message on MT not only didn't get any replies, as far as I can tell it didn't even reach a single other node. No acknowledgements, "max retransmissions reached". But on MC I saw two repeats immediately.
messages.content_html: I was surprised, considering the (allegedly) broader adoption of MT that my message on MT not only didn't get any replies, as far as I can tell it didn't even reach a single other node. No acknowledgements, "max retransmissions reached". But on MC I saw two repeats immediately.

messages.author: Mike / N2QDK
messages.text: Recently, Meshcore folks are getting dogpiled by the MT community when they say meshcore is objectively a better performing LoRa system. Better to let it do the talking.
messages.content_html: Recently, Meshcore folks are getting dogpiled by the MT community when they say meshcore is objectively a better performing LoRa system. Better to let it do the talking.

messages.author: Splicer
messages.text: I have some reservations about some of the reasons for the claims that MC is objectively better, but you know. The proof of the pudding is in the eating.
messages.content_html: I have some reservations about some of the reasons for the claims that MC is objectively better, but you know. The proof of the pudding is in the eating.

messages.author: Mike / N2QDK
messages.text: we encourage comparisons and talk of all LoRa types in the spirit of furthering technology
messages.content_html: we encourage comparisons and talk of all LoRa types in the spirit of furthering technology

messages.author: Mike / N2QDK
messages.text: like how?
messages.content_html: like how?
messages.reply_html:
Splicer
I have some reservations about some of the reasons for the claims that MC is objectively better, but you know. The proof of the pudding is in the eating.

messages.author: Splicer
messages.text: One of the arguments is that MT gets clogged up by constant sensor and device telemetry. MeshCore doesn't have as much telemetry support or sensor/device support built in to the downloadable firmware images. I take the point that being designed around and oriented toward human messaging makes the human messaging more reliable, but it comes down to the claim that it's better because we can do less stuff with it, so less traffic, less congestion.
messages.content_html: One of the arguments is that MT gets clogged up by constant sensor and device telemetry. MeshCore doesn't have as much telemetry support or sensor/device support built in to the downloadable firmware images. I take the point that being designed around and oriented toward human messaging makes the human messaging more reliable, but it comes down to the claim that it's better because we can do less stuff with it, so less traffic, less congestion.

messages.author: Mike / N2QDK
messages.text: that's not really true though. There is sensor support in meshcore
messages.content_html: that's not really true though. There is sensor support in meshcore

messages.author: Splicer
messages.text: Anyway, that aspect doesn't make it "objectively better". It's just a usage decision. The routing support OTOH, makes it objectively better.
messages.content_html: Anyway, that aspect doesn't make it "objectively better". It's just a usage decision. The routing support OTOH, makes it objectively better.

messages.author: Mike / N2QDK
messages.text: it's just not spamming the LoRa with that unsolicited data
messages.content_html: it's just not spamming the LoRa with that unsolicited data

messages.author: Splicer
messages.text: Sure. I 100% take the point that that decision might (and apparently does) make MeshCore more reliable. But it's a decision that the MT community could make too
messages.content_html: Sure. I 100% take the point that that decision might (and apparently does) make MeshCore more reliable. But it's a decision that the MT community could make too

messages.content_html: The nuances between the two approaches is really neat to me. (edited)

messages.author: Splicer
messages.text: Me too. Designing the system with the assumption that people should pair a "rooftop" node and a "user" node is a big deal. A frustration with MT is the 3 hop recommendation. I'm one of the "monsters" who set my MT hop limit to 7. When we make connections, they tend to be pretty short distances and the mesh isn't really dense enough to support reliable connections. And I live in fear of admitting that in any MT spaces because I'm "destroying the mesh"
messages.content_html: Me too. Designing the system with the assumption that people should pair a "rooftop" node and a "user" node is a big deal. A frustration with MT is the 3 hop recommendation. I'm one of the "monsters" who set my MT hop limit to 7. When we make connections, they tend to be pretty short distances and the mesh isn't really dense enough to support reliable connections. And I live in fear of admitting that in any MT spaces because I'm "destroying the mesh"

messages.author: Mike / N2QDK
messages.text: well, there's that. let's just say it's a different community vibe in MC
messages.content_html: well, there's that. let's just say it's a different community vibe in MC

messages.author: Splicer
messages.text: I should say: there are some kinda scoldy voices in that community. The MT folks on the whole are very helpful, supportive. I don't mean to say otherwise.
messages.content_html: I should say: there are some kinda scoldy voices in that community. The MT folks on the whole are very helpful, supportive. I don't mean to say otherwise.

messages.author: Mike / N2QDK
messages.text: Absolutely
messages.content_html: Absolutely

messages.author: Splicer
messages.text: With MT I'd just about given up on being able to get messages across the mesh, so I ordered a YAGI. I was gonna point it southeast in the direction of my makerspace and my friend who lives up the hill in Troy, see if that gets enough to make a conduit from there to here. I've had the YAGI for a few days and I'm waiting on an adapter to try it out. Still planning to test that with MT unless my friend has already set up an MC node. But after that I'll try to see if I can reach Maple Ridge and Thatcher Park from here even with the obstructions, by pointing the YAGI in the appropriate directions.
messages.content_html: With MT I'd just about given up on being able to get messages across the mesh, so I ordered a YAGI. I was gonna point it southeast in the direction of my makerspace and my friend who lives up the hill in Troy, see if that gets enough to make a conduit from there to here. I've had the YAGI for a few days and I'm waiting on an adapter to try it out. Still planning to test that with MT unless my friend has already set up an MC node. But after that I'll try to see if I can reach Maple Ridge and Thatcher Park from here even with the obstructions, by pointing the YAGI in the appropriate directions.

In theory it would only take one repeater on top of Stratton Mountain to reach my dad in White River Junction. Real world is not in theory… yeah, great minds on the Stratton thing.

messages.text: Surprisingly
messages.content_html: Surprisingly

messages.author: Splicer
messages.text: Well, I haven't analyzed the patterns and I don't have the math background to even try. It just seems like mountaintops in between active and well-populated meshes have the real possibility of being chokepoints. And the directional antennas should help with range. 1W is not a lot of power and even with perfect line of sight I assume you need good atmospheric conditions for mountaintop-to-mountaintop communications. Maybe I'm wrong about that?
messages.content_html: Well, I haven't analyzed the patterns and I don't have the math background to even try. It just seems like mountaintops in between active and well-populated meshes have the real possibility of being chokepoints. And the directional antennas should help with range. 1W is not a lot of power and even with perfect line of sight I assume you need good atmospheric conditions for mountaintop-to-mountaintop communications. Maybe I'm wrong about that?

messages.author: Mike / N2QDK

messages.author: Splicer

messages.content_html: a tenth of a watt? Wow. I'd assumed (without actually researching) all these nodes were pumping out close to the 1W limit

messages.text: only specialized hardware does that. the ESP repeaters are close to a watt because they have to overcome some attenuation right after the antenna

messages.reply_html:
Splicer
a tenth of a watt? Wow. I'd assumed (without actually researching) all these nodes were pumping out close to the 1W limit

messages.author: Splicer
messages.text: Unobstructed line of sight being more important than power or gain… won't that depend a lot on the actual distances? Inverse square law turns signals tiny over time. And atmospheric conditions are a real variable. I'm speaking from ignorance here, but those 150 and 200 mile mesh distance records aren't reliably repeatable. It's just my guesswork but I think in order to have reliable chatter over about 50 miles, even with perfect LOS you'd want every possible advantage.
messages.content_html: Unobstructed line of sight being more important than power or gain… won't that depend a lot on the actual distances? Inverse square law turns signals tiny over time. And atmospheric conditions are a real variable. I'm speaking from ignorance here, but those 150 and 200 mile mesh distance records aren't reliably repeatable. It's just my guesswork but I think in order to have reliable chatter over about 50 miles, even with perfect LOS you'd want every possible advantage.

messages.author: Mike / N2QDK
messages.text: you're correct, RF loss in free air exists, and up to about 50 miles it hasn't been much of a concern from what we've seen
messages.content_html: you're correct, RF loss in free air exists, and up to about 50 miles it hasn't been much of a concern from what we've seen

messages.author: Splicer
messages.text: In the long run I'd guess it would be better to have a repeater on every mountaintop than a few long hops. But if we want to bridge us to Boston I'm guessing we'd want to start with a line of big hops and then improve it by adding more mountaintops
messages.content_html: In the long run I'd guess it would be better to have a repeater on every mountaintop than a few long hops. But if we want to bridge us to Boston I'm guessing we'd want to start with a line of big hops and then improve it by adding more mountaintops

messages.author: Splicer
messages.text: I've talked to a lot of people at CoG about Mesh(tastic so far) and the response is almost universally, "seems cool. what can you do with it?" "Send text messages" is duplicating what people have (too much of) already. "Send text messages during the zombie apocalypse" is good but feels distant (we hope). "Text messages, including group messages like a BBS just for us here" feels like a real hook.
messages.content_html: I've talked to a lot of people at CoG about Mesh(tastic so far) and the response is almost universally, "seems cool. what can you do with it?" "Send text messages" is duplicating what people have (too much of) already. "Send text messages during the zombie apocalypse" is good but feels distant (we hope). "Text messages, including group messages like a BBS just for us here" feels like a real hook.

messages.author: Splicer
messages.text: As a member I can reserve the community room and host an event, even for non-members. It could be put on the public calendar for the makerspace. I think it would have to be listed as "free for members, suggested donation for non-members". Never anyone at the door asking for the suggested donation but I think that's what it's supposed to say for groups listed on their calendar as public. But the group wouldn't have to pay for the room as long as there's at least one person who is a member.
